title: Broadsheet fold zine from A3 paper
subtitle: An eight-page A6 zine from one sheet of A3 with a single cut
excerpt: A single A3 sheet, folded and cut in a specific sequence, produces an eight-page A6 zine with no adhesive. The same one-sheet-one-cut structure that works on A4 paper scales up on A3 to give a slightly larger and more substantial page.
sourceNotes: The one-sheet folded zine structure derives from paper-folding geometry documented widely in paper-engineering and zine-making guides. No pre-1928 public-domain source specifically documents this construction; the fold geometry is synthesised from general paper-folding logic.

glossaryTerms.definition: The assembled pages of a book before or after binding. In a one-sheet zine, the book block is formed by folding and is not a separate assembly step.
The pages that sit at the middle of a saddle-stapled or folded zine; in a standard eight-page booklet, pages 4 and 5. In the one-sheet fold, the centre is where the single releasing cut is made.
The series of folds at the spine edge of a folded booklet. In the one-sheet construction, the pages fan out from the accordion spine when the booklet is spread open.

content.text: The one-sheet zine is one of the most satisfying paper constructions: a single sheet, folded in a specific order and cut in one place, opens into a fully formed eight-page booklet. No staples, no adhesive, no binding. The fold geometry works on any sheet size. On A3, the resulting pages are A6; a size that sits well in the hand, photographs easily, and can be written or drawn on at a comfortable scale.

items.name: A3 paper, 80–90 gsm
items.substitutions: Any uncoated paper from 70 to 100 gsm works. Lighter papers fold without resistance but feel flimsy; heavier papers resist the fold at the spine and can crack. 80 gsm printer paper is the most available option.

items.name: Bone folder
items.substitutions: The edge of a credit card in a pinch, but a bone folder produces a sharper, flatter crease.

items.name: Craft knife
items.substitutions: Scissors work for the single cut but produce a less precise edge.

content.text: Laying out the pages before folding

If you are filling the zine with content before folding, lay the sheet landscape (long edge horizontal) and plan the eight panels. Working from left to right on one side: panel 8 (back cover), panel 1 (front cover), panel 2, panel 3. Flip the sheet over and work from right to left: panel 4, panel 5, panel 6, panel 7. The page order is counter-intuitive before folding; make a small folded test sheet from scrap paper first to see which panels land where.
For a blank zine, skip the layout step. The structure works regardless of whether pages are filled first or after folding.
The fold sequence

Lay the sheet landscape. Fold in half along the long axis: bring the top edge down to meet the bottom edge. Press the fold flat with the bone folder. You now have a landscape sheet of half the original height.
Fold in half again along the short axis: bring the right edge across to meet the left edge. Press flat. You have a portrait folded sheet at A4 size.

Fold in half once more along the short axis: bring the right edge across to meet the left edge again. Press flat with the bone folder, working from the spine outward to push any trapped air out. You now have a portrait packet at A6 size; the page size of the finished zine.
The single cut
Open the A6 packet back to the A5 stage: the last fold only. You are holding a folded A5 sheet with a fold at the left and an open edge at the right. You can see the
centre pages
; the two-page spread in the middle of the booklet.

The cut goes along the fold at the left edge, but only through the centre half. Lay the folded sheet on the cutting mat with the fold at the left. The full fold runs from the top of the sheet to the bottom. You need to cut from the midpoint of the fold (the centre of the sheet) to the open end at the right; specifically, cut only through the first fold.

More precisely: with the A5 folded sheet in front of you, stand it up so the fold is horizontal at the top. Cut along the top fold from the centre point to the right-hand open edge. This cut goes through one layer of paper only, opening the two centre pages so they can spread apart when the zine is formed.
Use the craft knife against the ruler for a clean cut. Scissors work but may leave a slight bow in the cut line.
Forming the book block

Unfold the sheet fully. Fold it in half along the long axis as in step one. Hold the sheet with both hands at the centre, with the cut opening facing you. Push the two ends gently toward each other. The cut opening spreads apart like a mouth opening, and the sheet folds into a star shape, then collapses into a flat
book block
. Press the spine flat with the bone folder. The
accordion spine
folds to the left. Crease all the page edges firmly.
The result is an eight-page booklet: front cover, six inside pages, and a back cover, all from one sheet with one cut.
Finishing
Press the finished zine under a flat weight for ten minutes. This sets the page creases flat. The zine stays closed naturally; no staple is needed.
Care

The one-sheet zine is inherently robust because every page is part of the same continuous sheet. It survives in pockets and bags better than a stapled zine, where the staple can pull through at the spine. The only structural weak point is the cut edge at the centre spread; avoid pulling pages open at that edge with force.
Variations

The same construction on A4 paper produces an eight-page A7 zine; smaller and more portable. On A2 paper it produces an eight-page A5 zine with a more substantial feel. The fold sequence is identical regardless of sheet size.

Printing two sheets and nesting one inside the other before folding gives a sixteen-page zine of the same page size. The nested sheets do not need to be attached before folding; the fold and the weight of the pages holds them together. A long-arm stapler driven through the spine at the centre fold secures the nested sheets permanently.
